title: Home Page
text: The Home Page serves as the primary navigation hub for the MGnify Biosynthetic Gene Clusters. This page provides quick access to the main functionalities of the portal, allowing users to search for BGCs, access API documentation, find user guides, and learn more about the project.

title: Home Page
text:
Keyword search
At the top of every page, you will find the Keyword Search bar. This feature allows you to quickly perform a basic search across the portal data and documents using specific keywords or accessions. This functionality is designed to provide a fast and straightforward way to retrieve data without requiring advanced search criteria.

title: Home Page
text:
Access and explore options
The Home Page offers several options to access and explore metagenomic BGC data:
Explore BGCs gives access to more detailed searches using advanced criteria. This feature is particularly useful for users who need to perform complex queries or refine their search results based on specific parameters. Also provides basic statistics on the data portal alongside the detailed results of their searches.
API Access section provides documentation for users who wish to programmatically interact with the portal. Review the documentation, which includes endpoint descriptions and parameter details.
User Documentation section is where you will find guides on how to use the portal.
About section provides background information on the portal and the team behind its development.
This page is access point to exploring and utilising the MGnify Biosynthetic Gene Clusters portal.

title: BGC Details
text: This section provides a guide on how to explore the BGC details page within the MGnify Biosynthetic Gene Clusters portal. Users can gain insights into the aggregated regions, understand various annotations, and download relevant data files.

title: BGC Details
text:
Overview section
At the top of the BGC details page, users will find summary information and related datasets about the aggregated region. This includes:


Sample information

Assembly accession: The ENA (European Nucleotide Archive) assembly accession associated with the predicted BGC region. This is a unique identifier for the assembly from which the BGC was predicted. The assembly accession is linked to the MGnify assembly page, where users can explore the assembly in more detail.
Biome: Information about the biome from which the assembly originates. Biome data provides context on the environmental source of the DNA sequence, which can be important for understanding the ecological relevance of the BGC.
Contig information: This includes contig accession (MGYC) where the BGC was predicted, as well as the start and end positions of the aggregated region.



Aggregated region

Predicted classes: Provides the BGC class detected by each method.
Functional annotation: Shows the description of the GOslim terms found in the aggregated region.

title: BGC Details
text:
Explore region

Region plot
The BGC plot is a visual representation of the predicted BGC within the contig. It includes several layers of information:


Coding Regions (CDS): Displayed as white arrows with black edges, each arrow represents a coding sequence within the contig. The direction of the arrow indicates the strand on which the gene is located. Users can click on these arrows to reveal additional CDS data, including protein sequence and Pfam annotations.
Pfam annotations: Pfam domains within the CDS are represented by colored arrows inside the white CDS arrows. The color corresponds to the Gene Ontology (GO) slim molecular function associated with that Pfam. Clicking reveals additional CDS data, including links to the Pfan entries in InterPro.
BGC predictions: Horizontal colored bars at the bottom of the plot represent BGC predictions by individual detection tools.
Aggregated region: An unfilled horizontal bar at the bottom of the plot represents the aggregated BGC region. This region encompasses the consensus prediction from all specified detectors, and its coordinates are reflected in the results table.

Hovering the cursor over the elements will display accession and other information.


Protein information

When users click on a coding sequence (CDS) in the plot, detailed information about the CDS is displayed:

Protein sequence: The amino acid sequence of the protein encoded by the CDS. This sequence is crucial for further functional analysis or comparisons with other sequences.
Pfam details: Information about the Pfam domain(s) within the CDS, including descriptions and associated GO terms. This helps in understanding the potential function of the protein.
Representative protein: A link to the representative protein in the MGnify proteins database. This link provides additional information about the protein, including its structure, biome distribution, and other relevant annotations.

title: BGC Details
text:
Download options
The BGC details page offers download options for users who wish to retrieve data for further analysis:

GeneBank: This file contains the nucleotide sequence of the aggregated BGC region, along with annotated features such as predicted genes, translation sequences, Pfam domains, and BGC region information. The GeneBank format is widely used for storing detailed sequence data, and it is compatible with various bioinformatics tools.
JSON: The JSON file is formatted according to antiSMASH specifications and can be used directly within the antiSMASH ecosystem. This file is useful for users who want to perform further analysis using antiSMASH tools or integrate the data into their existing antiSMASH workflows.
FASTA: This file provides the nucleotide sequence of the aggregated BGC region in FASTA format. FASTA is a simple, widely-used format for representing nucleotide sequences, making it easy to use with various sequence analysis tools.


These download options allow users to take the predicted BGC data into their preferred analysis pipelines, ensuring flexibility and compatibility with different bioinformatics platforms.

title: Explore BGCs
text:
The Advanced Search bar allows you to perform fine-grained queries to explore BGC Data Portal. Below is a detailed explanation of each search field and examples of complex search queries to help you utilise this feature effectively.



BGC Class Name:
Enter one or more BGC class names (i.e., alkaloid, nrp, other, polyketide, ripp, saccharide, or terpene) separated by spaces or commas. The search will return BGCs that contain at least one of the specified classes (using an OR logic).
MGYB:
Specify a particular MGYB (BGC accession of portal) to directly retrieve data associated with that BGC.
Assembly Accession:
Input an ENA assembly accession to find predicted BGCs within that specific assembly.
Contig MGYC:
Provide a specific MGYC (contig accession) to locate BGCs within that contig.
Biome:
Enter terms related to the biome lineage (e.g., sediment, hydrothermal vent) to filter BGCs that originate from assemblies associated with those biomes.
BGC Completeness:
Select from the following options to refine your search based on the BGCs’ contig boundaries:

Full-Length BGC: The BGC is entirely within the contig, meaning both start and end positions are within the boundaries of the contig, indicating a complete prediction.
Single: Partial BGC (Single edge). The BGC extends to one end of the contig, suggesting that the prediction may be truncated at one side because the contig ends there.
Double: Partial BGC (Double edges). The BGC spans the entire length of the contig, indicating that the prediction is likely truncated on both sides, with both ends of the BGC coinciding with the ends of the contig.

Pfam:
Enter one or more Pfam accessions (e.g., PF00109, PF00501) separated by spaces or commas. You can define the strategy to search BGCs containing these Pfams:

OR: The resulting BGCs have at least one of the specified Pfams.
AND: The resulting BGCs must include all specified Pfams.

Pfam strategy:
The Pfam strategy field determines how the search will interpret multiple Pfam entries. You have two options:

OR logic: When you select the OR strategy, the search will return BGCs that contain at least one of the Pfams you specified. This is useful when you are interested in BGCs that have any of a list of potential Pfams, allowing for a broader search. For example, if you enter PF00109 PF00501 and select OR, the search will return BGCs that contain either PF00109, PF00501, or both.
AND logic: Selecting the AND strategy will narrow the search results to BGCs that must contain all the Pfams you listed. This is ideal when you are looking for BGCs with a specific combination of Pfams. For example, if you enter PF00109 PF00501 and choose AND, only those BGCs that contain both PF00109 and PF00501 will be returned.

BGC Detectors: Filter BGCs detected by the supported detectors:

antiSMASH
GECCO
SanntiS

Aggregate strategy: The Aggregate Strategy determines how BGC regions predicted by different detection tools are combined in the final results. This field is important when you want to either view individual predictions or identify consensus regions supported by multiple tools. There are three strategies to choose from:

Single:
This strategy displays the predictions from each tool separately, even if they overlap. When using this option, you might see multiple entries for the same region if different tools predicted similar regions. This strategy is useful for comparing how different tools view the same region but does not combine their outputs into a single prediction.
Union:
The Union strategy merges regions where at least one gene overlaps across predictions of all selected BGC detectors. The resulting aggregated region will be defined by the outermost start and end positions of these overlapping predictions. This strategy creates a broader region that represents all potential BGCs detected by at least one of the selected tools, providing a more inclusive view of the data. It is useful when you want to ensure that you capture all possible BGCs, even if only partially supported by some tools.  This region was aggregated selecting SanntiS and GECCO BGC detectors.
Intersection:
The Intersection strategy is the most stringent. It only includes regions where all the selected BGC detectors agree. The resulting BGC region will be the longest contiguous sequence that all tools have predicted as a BGC. This strategy is particularly useful when you want high-confidence predictions, as it guarantees that the region is supported by multiple independent tools, reducing the likelihood of false positives.  This region was aggregated selecting antiSMASH and GECCO BGC detectors. Note that despite SanntiS BGC being displayed, it was not considered for the aggregated region

title: Explore BGCs
text:
Results table
After conducting a search, the results will be displayed in a paginated table. Below are key components of the results summary and table, along with instructions for downloading and interpreting the data.

Results summary

Number of Aggregated Regions

Number of studies where regions matching the criteria where found
Number of assemblies where regions matching the criteria where found
Class Distribution. Note that for regions with more than one class predicted only one will be counted.



Table columns

MGYB: The BGC accession (or aggregated accession) for the resulting region.
Assembly: The ENA assembly accession number where the BGC was predicted.
MGYC: The accession of the contig containing the BGC.
Start: The start coordinate of the BGC within the contig.
End: The end coordinate of the BGC within the contig.
Detectors: Lists the BGC detectors that contributed to the aggregated prediction.
Classes: The predicted BGC classes within the aggregated region.
Details: Link to the BGC details page.



Downloading search results
You can download the search results as a TSV file, which can be used in conjunction with the API to retrieve the data locally.

title: API Documentation
text: For detailed information on how to use the API, including available endpoints and request formats, please refer to the comprehensive API documentation available on the Portal’s Home page under the “API Access” section.

title: API Documentation
text:
What is an API?
An API (Application Programming Interface) allows different software applications to communicate with each other. In the context of the MGnify Biosynthetic Gene Clusters Portal, the API provides a programmatic way for users to access and interact with the portal’s data. This can be especially useful for automating workflows, integrating the portal’s data with other bioinformatics tools, or performing large-scale analyses without needing to use the web interface.

title: Introduction
text:
Biosynthetic gene clusters are important for understanding the production of natural products, including antibiotics and other bioactive compounds. The MGnify Biosynthetic Gene Clusters portal provides a resource for researchers who need access to predicted biosynthetic gene clusters from a wide range of metagenomic datasets from MGnify.
The MGnify Biosynthetic Gene Clusters is a platform designed to facilitate the exploration, analysis, and retrieval of biosynthetic gene cluster predictions from metagenomic assemblies.
The portal integrates data from multiple prediction tools, enabling users to perform detailed searches, visualise results, and download data in various formats.

title: Introduction
text:
Basic navigation
The MGnify Biosynthetic Gene Clusters Portal is designed with three primary components that help users navigate and utilise its features effectively. Below is an overview of these components:

1. Home page
The Home Page is your entry point to the MGnify Biosynthetic Gene Clusters portal. Here, you can navigate to different sections of the site, perform a quick keyword search, and access key functionalities. The main features of the Home Page include:

Keyword search: Located at the top of the page, this allows you to search the portal using specific keywords or accession numbers, retrieving relevant records from both documentation and data. This is equivalent to calling the GET /api/v1/keyword_search/ endpoint of the API.
Access and explore options:

Search BGCs: Directs you to the Explore BGCs page for more detailed queries.
API Access: Provides documentation on how to use the API for programmatic access to BGC data.
User Documentation: Guides and examples to help you navigate and utilise the portal effectively.
Downloads: Access the FTP site to download the latest data.
About Us: Information about the project, including details on the EUREMAP initiative supporting marine bioprospecting research.




2. Explore BGCs page
The Explore BGCs page is where users can perform detailed searches and view the results. It includes:

Advanced search sidebar: This allows for complex queries using various criteria, including BGC class names, specific accessions, biome data, and more. This is equivalent to calling the GET /api/v1/advanced_search/ endpoint of the API.
Search results: Shows summary statistics of the search.
Results table: Displays the outcomes of your search in a paginated format, with options to download the data in TSV. The table includes key details such as BGC accession, assembly accession, contig information, and detector names. This results are equivalent to the results of calling the GET /api/v1/advanced_search/ or GET /api/v1/keyword_search/ endpoints of the API.



3. BGC Details page
The BGC Details Page provides an in-depth view of a specific biosynthetic gene cluster. Key features include:

Aggregated region overview: Displays essential details such as assembly accession, contig information, biome data, and the BGC classes predicted.
BGC Plot: A visual representation of the BGC, including coding regions, Pfam annotations, and predictions from various detectors. Hovering and clicking elements display further information.
CDS Data: Detailed information about coding sequences, including protein sequences, Pfam descriptions, and links to external resources.
Download options: Allows you to download data related to the BGC in formats GeneBank, JSON, and FASTA. This is equivalent to calling the GET /api/v1/contig_region/ endpoint of the API using the MGYC and start/end positions of the aggregated region.

title: Introduction
text:
Glossary of terms
Below are definitions of key terms and concepts used in the MGnify Biosynthetic Gene Clusters portal:

Biosynthetic Gene Cluster (BGC): A group of genes that collectively are predicted to produce a secondary metabolite by a specific BGC detector, often with ecological or pharmaceutical significance.
Aggregated region: In the context of the MGnify Biosynthetic Gene Clusters portal, an aggregated region refers to a genomic region that is identified as a BGC through the combined predictions of multiple BGC detection tools. The aggregation process involves comparing the predictions from different detectors and merging them based on specific strategies (i.e., single, union, intersection). The resulting aggregated region represents the consensus prediction, which may span across overlapping regions identified by the different tools, ensuring a comprehensive and reliable identification of the BGC.
MGYB: A specific accession number related to each BGC predictions within the portal.
Aggregate strategy: The method used to combine predictions from multiple BGC detectors. This can be done using single, union, or intersection strategies to determine the final aggregated region. See Search fields for detailed information.

For more detailed instructions, please refer to the specific sections of the documentation.

title: Getting Started
text:
The MGnify Biosynthetic Gene Clusters Portal is designed with three primary components that help users navigate and utilise its features effectively. Below is an overview of these components:


The Home Page is your entry point to the MGnify Biosynthetic Gene Clusters portal. Here, you can navigate to different sections of the site, perform a quick keyword search, and access key functionalities. The main features of the Home Page include:

Keyword Search: Located at the top of the page, this allows you to search the portal using specific keywords or accession numbers, retrieving relevant records from both documentation and data. This is equivalent to calling the GET /api/v1/search/ endpoint of the API.
Access and Explore Options:

Search BGCs: Directs you to the Explore BGCs page for more detailed queries.
API Access: Provides documentation on how to use the API for programmatic access to BGC data.
User Documentation: Guides and examples to help you navigate and utilise the portal effectively.
About Us: Information about the project, including details on the EUREMAP initiative supporting marine bioprospecting research.





The Explore BGCs Page is where users can perform detailed searches and view the results. It includes:

Advanced Search Sidebar: This allows for complex queries using various criteria, including BGC class names, specific accessions, biome data, and more. This is equivalent to calling the GET /api/v1/bgcs/ endpoint of the API.
Results Table: Displays the outcomes of your search in a paginated format, with options to download the data in TSV. The table includes key details such as BGC accession, assembly accession, contig information, and detector names. This results are equivalent to the results of calling the GET /api/v1/search/ or GET /api/v1/bgcs/ endpoints of the API.




The BGC Details Page provides an in-depth view of a specific biosynthetic gene cluster. Key features include:

Aggregated Region Basic Information: Displays essential details such as assembly accession, contig information, biome data, and the BGC classes predicted.
BGC Plot: A visual representation of the BGC, including coding regions, Pfam annotations, and predictions from various detectors.
CDS Data: Detailed information about coding sequences, including protein sequences, Pfam descriptions, and links to external resources.
Download Options: Allows you to download data related to the BGC in various formats, including GeneBank, JSON, FASTA, and GFF3. This is equivalent to calling the GET /api/v1/contig_region/ endpoint of the API using the MGYC and start/end positions of the aggregated region.

title: Getting Started
text:
Glossary of Terms
Below are definitions of key terms and concepts used in the MGnify Biosynthetic Gene Clusters portal:

Biosynthetic Gene Cluster (BGC): A group of genes that collectively are predicted to produce a secondary metabolite by a specific BGC detector, often with ecological or pharmaceutical significance.
Aggregated Region: In the context of the MGnify Biosynthetic Gene Clusters portal, an aggregated region refers to a genomic region that is identified as a BGC through the combined predictions of multiple BGC detection tools. The aggregation process involves comparing the predictions from different detectors and merging them based on specific strategies (i.e., single, union, intersection). The resulting aggregated region represents the consensus prediction, which may span across overlapping regions identified by the different tools, ensuring a comprehensive and reliable identification of the BGC.
MGYB: A specific accession number related to each BGC predictions within the portal.
Aggregate Strategy: The method used to combine predictions from multiple BGC detectors. This can be done using single, union, or intersection strategies to determine the final predicted BGC regions.

For more detailed instructions, please refer to the specific sections of the documentation.

title: About
text:
MGnify Biosynthetic Gene Clusters is a platform developed to support the exploration, analysis, and retrieval of biosynthetic gene cluster (BGC) predictions from metagenomic assemblies. This resource is designed to advance natural product research by making standardised BGC predictions accessible to the scientific community without requiring extensive computational resources. The platform integrates multiple detection tools, including antiSMASH, GECCO, and SanntiS, into a standardised pipeline that ensures comparability of results across studies. By offering multi-tool analyses, users can easily identify consensus predictions, enhancing the reliability and insight gained from the data. The platform is intended for both technical and non-technical users, providing analytics features for general use and an API for more advanced data interactions.
MGnify Biosynthetic Gene Clusters aims to make this valuable dataset widely accessible, promoting advances in natural product science across research, industry, and the broader community. The portal is part of MGnify, which offers automated pipelines for the analysis and archiving of microbiome data. MGnify helps determine the taxonomic diversity and functional and metabolic potential of environmental samples. Users can submit their data for analysis or browse the publicly available datasets within the repository. Additionally, MGnify can analyse datasets from the European Nucleotide Archive (ENA) upon request.

title: About
text:
Dataset overview
This dataset offers a comprehensive collection of biosynthetic gene cluster (BGC) predictions derived from MGnify metagenomic assemblies, utilising tools such as antiSMASH [1], GECCO [2], and SanntiS [3]. The data is organised within an SQLite database, containing details on BGCs, including associated contigs, assemblies, proteins, and relevant metadata.
The dataset is generated through a pipeline that integrates these detection tools, providing BGC predictions from metagenomic assemblies. The pipeline is openly available.

title: About
text:
Funding and support
This portal has been developed at EMBL-EBI by the MGnify team as part of the EUREMAP project. EUREMAP is a European initiative designed to create an integrated pipeline of marine bioprospecting services to discover novel bioactive natural products from marine organisms. This project connects research infrastructures, industry, and academia strengthening the blue economy.
EUREMAP project is funded by the European Union under HORIZON-INFRA-2023-DEV-01-04 (Grant No. 101131663).

title: About
text:
Contact
MGnify Biosynthetic Gene Clusters is currently in beta and under active development. We value feedback and encourage users to share any issues or suggestions via our contact form.

title: About
text:
References
[1] Blin, Kai, Simon Shaw, Hannah E. Augustijn, Zachary L. Reitz, Friederike Biermann, Mohammad Alanjary, Artem Fetter et al. “antiSMASH 7.0: new and improved predictions for detection, regulation, chemical structures and visualisation.” Nucleic acids research 51, no. W1 (2023): W46-W50.
[2] Carroll, Laura M., Martin Larralde, Jonas Simon Fleck, Ruby Ponnudurai, Alessio Milanese, Elisa Cappio, and Georg Zeller. “Accurate de novo identification of biosynthetic gene clusters with GECCO.” BioRxiv (2021): 2021-05.
[3] Sanchez, Santiago, Joel D. Rogers, Alexander B. Rogers, Maaly Nassar, Johanna McEntyre, Martin Welch, Florian Hollfelder, and Robert D. Finn. “Expansion of novel biosynthetic gene clusters from diverse environments using SanntiS.” bioRxiv (2023): 2023-05.
